 return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Request body is empty' });
 }
 // Process valid JSON body
 res.json({ received: req.body });
});

Handle Content-Type Mismatch

app.use(express.json({
 type: ['application/json', 'application/vnd.api+json']
}));

app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
 if (err.type === 'parse') {
 return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid JSON in request body' });
 }
 next(err);
});

Test with curl

# Valid JSON
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/data \
 -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
 -d '{"name": "test", "value": 42}'

# Invalid JSON (missing closing bracket)
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/data \
 -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
 -d '{"name": "test"'

Related Errors

  • Express Validation Error — input validation failure
  • Express 404 Route Not Found — undefined route

item.title: JsonSlurper error in Groovy
item.description:
What This Error Means

Groovy’s JsonSlurper parses JSON strings and files into maps and lists. Parse errors occur when the JSON is malformed, has trailing commas, or contains invalid syntax.

Common Causes

  • Trailing commas in JSON (not valid JSON)
  • Single quotes instead of double quotes
  • Unquoted keys
  • Comments in JSON (not valid)

How to Fix

// WRONG: Trailing comma
def json = '{"name": "Alice", "age": 30,}'

// CORRECT: No trailing comma
def json = '{"name": "Alice", "age": 30}'

// WRONG: Single quotes for keys
def json = "{'name': 'Alice'}"

// CORRECT: Double quotes
def json = '{"name": "Alice"}'

Examples

def jsonStr = '{"name": "Alice", "scores": [95, 87, 92]}'
def parsed = new JsonSlurper().parseText(jsonStr)
println parsed.name // Alice
println parsed.scores[0] // 95

Related Errors

  • XmlSlurper Error - XML parsing issues

item.title: Serialization error
item.description:
This error occurs when Spring (via Jackson) cannot serialize a Java object to JSON or deserialize JSON into a Java object. This commonly affects REST API responses and request body parsing.

Common Causes

  • Circular references between objects (e.g. bidirectional JPA relationships)
  • Missing default constructor on a DTO class
  • Jackson cannot serialize a field (e.g. InputStream, Proxy)
  • JSON does not match the target class structure

How to Fix

  1. Break circular references with @JsonIgnore or @JsonManagedReference/@JsonBackReference:
@Entity
public class User {
 @OneToMany(mappedBy = "user")
 @JsonManagedReference
 private List<Post> posts;
}

@Entity
public class Post {
 @ManyToOne
 @JsonBackReference
 private User user;
}
  1. Ensure DTOs have a no-arg constructor:
public class UserDto {
 private String name;
 private String email;

 // Required for Jackson deserialization
 public UserDto() {}

 public UserDto(String name, String email) {
 this.name = name;
 this.email = email;
 }
}
  1. Handle InvalidDefinitionException globally:
@RestControllerAdvice
public class JsonExceptionHandler {
 @ExceptionHandler(HttpMessageNotReadableException.class)
 public ResponseEntity<Map<String, String>> handleJsonError(HttpMessageNotReadableException ex) {
 return ResponseEntity.badRequest().body(Map.of("error", "Invalid JSON: " + ex.getMessage()));
 }
}

Examples

// Bidirectional JPA relationship causes infinite recursion
@Entity
public class Department {
 @OneToMany(mappedBy = "department")
 private List<Employee> employees; // Employee has Department reference
}

// Response: {"employees":[{"department":{"employees":[...
// Error: Infinite recursion (StackOverflow)

com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.InvalidDefinitionException:
No serializer found for class org.hibernate.proxy... and no properties discovered

Related Errors

  • Profile not found

item.title: SyntaxError: Unexpected token in JSON
item.description:
This error occurs when Express (via body-parser) receives a request with a Content-Type: application/json header but the body contains malformed JSON.

Common Causes

  • Malformed JSON in the request body (missing commas, extra quotes)
  • Request body is empty or raw text when JSON was expected
  • body-parser middleware not configured
  • Using wrong Content-Type header in the client

How to Fix

  1. Add body-parser middleware with error handling:
const express = require('express');
const app = express();

app.use(express.json({ limit: '10mb' }));
app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true }));

// Global JSON error handler
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
 if (err.type === 'entity.parse.failed') {
 return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid JSON' });
 }
 next(err);
});
  1. Validate JSON on the client before sending:
// Client-side validation
const data = { name: "Alice" };
const json = JSON.stringify(data);
fetch('/api/users', {
 method: 'POST',
 headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
 body: json
});
  1. Set a body size limit to reject oversized payloads:
app.use(express.json({ limit: '1mb' }));

Examples

// Sending malformed JSON
fetch('/api/users', {
 method: 'POST',
 headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
 body: '{"name": "Alice", "age": }' // missing value
})

SyntaxError: Unexpected token } in JSON at position 23
at JSON.parse (<anonymous>)

Related Errors

  • Payload too large

item.title: type X is not a subtype of type Y
item.description:
The “type X is not a subtype of type Y” error occurs in Dart when you try to assign or cast a value to an incompatible type. This is most common when parsing JSON data or working with dynamic types in Dart’s type system.

Common Causes

  • JSON values parsed as wrong types (e.g., int parsed as String)
  • Unsafe type casting with as operator
  • Deserializing API responses without type validation
  • List or Map types not matching expected generic types
  • Null values cast to non-nullable types

How to Fix

  1. Use safe type casting:
// Bad: unsafe cast
final count = data['count'] as int;

// Good: safe cast with null check
final count = (data['count'] as num?)?.toInt() ?? 0;
  1. Create a typed model with factory constructor:
class User {
 final String name;
 final int age;

 User({required this.name, required this.age});

 factory User.fromJson(Map<String, dynamic> json) {
 return User(
 name: json['name'] as String? ?? '',
 age: (json['age'] as num?)?.toInt() ?? 0,
 );
 }
}

final user = User.fromJson(responseData);
  1. Use pattern matching for type checking (Dart 3+):
final result = switch (data) {
 {'name': String name, 'age': int age} => User(name: name, age: age),
 _ => User(name: 'Unknown', age: 0),
};
  1. Handle dynamic JSON safely:
Map<String, dynamic> parseJson(String jsonString) {
 final decoded = jsonDecode(jsonString);
 if (decoded is Map<String, dynamic>) {
 return decoded;
 }
 throw FormatException('Invalid JSON format');
}
  1. Use generic type constraints:
T safeCast<T>(dynamic value, T defaultValue) {
 if (value is T) return value;
 return defaultValue;
}

final name = safeCast<String>(json['name'], 'Unknown');

Examples

// Error: type 'String' is not a subtype of type 'int'
final data = {'count': '5'};
final count = data['count'] as int; // String cannot be cast to int

// Fix: parse correctly
final count = int.parse(data['count'] as String);

// Or handle both types
final count = (data['count'] as num?)?.toInt() ?? 0;

Related Errors

  • Null error
  • Index error
